artifacts.sourceTitle: Considering Generative AI at Virginia Tech
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Faculty members in all disciplines need to understand how generative AI tools work, consider how they might enhance the student learning experience, recognize the potential risks associated with these tools, and establish carefully considered course policies to ensure they are used in pedagogically sound ways.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: TLOS frames GenAI as a course-policy issue and recommends carefully considered course policies.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Tools & Access at Virginia Tech
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Virginia Tech provides access to vetted AI tools and platforms that align with our AI principles and governance framework. All tools undergo evaluation for security, privacy, effectiveness, and alignment with university values before approval for campus use. Note: If an AI tool is not explicitly approved, it may only be used with low-risk, public data.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Virginia Tech describes vetted AI tools and limits unapproved AI tools to low-risk public data.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The Office of Undergraduate Academic Integrity encourages all faculty to state a clear policy on student use of generative AI for each class syllabus. Below are three possible policy statements, from permitting to prohibiting the use of generative AI in class, which can be adapted as needed.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The Honor System page encourages class-level generative AI policies and provides examples ranging from permitted to prohibited use.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Be judicious when sharing university data with publicly available AI tools
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: We ask that all members of our community not share any personal, sensitive or high-risk data in publicly available AI platforms (e.g. ChatGPT.com, DeepSeek.com). When such data is shared with open and publicly available AI tools, it can expose individuals and organizations to security, privacy, copyright, and intellectual property risks.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The IT notice asks community members not to share personal, sensitive, or high-risk data in public AI platforms.

artifacts.title: Virginia Tech Generative AI Guidance Staged Review
artifacts.summary: Virginia Tech has official public guidance covering teaching and syllabus expectations, vetted AI tools and data-risk handling, responsible AI use in research, undergraduate academic-integrity framing, and public AI data-sharing cautions. This staged run uses only official Virginia Tech sources and keeps program-specific or lower-specificity official candidates out of the claim set.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
OpenClaw staged candidates are not canonical public claims and require downstream review before publication.
The run did not access login-required Virginia Tech services or 4Help content; public official pages were sufficient for the staged candidate set.
